01_initial_request.input_data: GNSS positioning data containing multiple outliers
01_initial_request.user_intent: To propose a unified Detection, Identification, and Adaptation (DIA) framework based on Maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate to handle multiple outliers effectively
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Achieve superior True Positive Rate (TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) compared to conventional methods

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The DIA-datasnooping method is commonly used for outlier detection in GNSS data but is limited when dealing with multiple outliers. [Gap] A unified framework is needed that can handle both single and multiple outliers effectively. [Decision] To achieve a unified DIA process, chose to use Bayesian inference framework to perform prior distribution setup for gross errors on observational model, expecting to obtain probabilistic description of outlier patterns.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Prior distribution of gross errors established with pattern matrix Z (diagonal binary values) and size vector ∇, where each diagonal element follows Bernoulli distribution with parameter ε

references.relevance: Provides foundation for Bayesian approach to outlier problems
Supports use of non-informative priors for gross error size

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] With prior distributions established, the DIA framework needs an estimation method to select optimal hypotheses. [Gap] Conventional methods rely on test statistics comparison which becomes irrational for multiple outliers. [Decision] To achieve optimal hypothesis selection, chose to use Maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate to perform posterior probability maximization on hypothesis space, expecting to obtain optimal model with maximum posterior probability.

parameters.optimization_objective: maximize posterior probability distribution p(H_i, ∇_i, x|y)
parameters.posterior_calculation: product of likelihood and prior distribution

02_agent_trajectory.observation: MAP estimate equations derived (equations 31-33), showing that among hypotheses with same dimensions, model selection by DIA-MAP and UMPI test yields identical results, and estimated unknown parameter equals BLUE

references.relevance: Classic references for MAP estimate in Bayesian statistics

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The DIA-MAP method requires a reliable prior gross error rate ε as hyperparameter. [Gap] The true gross error rate is unknown in actual applications. [Decision] To achieve optimal hyperparameter estimation, chose to use supervised learning to perform loss function minimization on training data, expecting to obtain optimal estimated ε̂ that minimizes positioning RMSE.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Optimal ε̂ found that minimizes average RMSE; RMSE not sensitive to small deviation of ε̂ within certain interval; optimal ε̂ usually decreases as gross error size increases

references.relevance: Machine learning reference for supervised learning approach

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Conventional evaluation methods for single outlier are impractical for multiple outliers with unknown numbers. [Gap] Need appropriate metrics to assess DIA method performance for multiple outliers. [Decision] To achieve comprehensive performance evaluation, chose to use confusion matrix metrics to perform TPR and TNR calculation on outlier detection results, expecting to obtain detectability and acceptance ability measures.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: TPR describes correct detection rate for outliers (ratio of correctly rejected outlier number to total outlier number); TNR describes correct acceptance rate for inliers (ratio of correctly accepted inlier number to total inlier number)

references.relevance: Standard references for evaluation metrics in statistics

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The DIA-MAP method needs validation under controlled conditions with known parameters. [Gap] Performance under different gross error rates and sizes needs systematic evaluation. [Decision] To achieve performance validation, chose to use Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) to perform independent experiments on GNSS single point positioning, expecting to obtain TPR, TNR, and RMSE values under various conditions.

parameters.gross_error_sizes: 1 to 20 times STD of corresponding observation

02_agent_trajectory.observation: DIA-MAP consistently achieves highest TPR regardless of rate and size of gross errors; TNR of DIA-MAP declines first then increases to stable point between DIA-datasnooping and iterative DIA-datasnooping; positioning errors using DIA-MAP generally much smaller and can always be controlled

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] After validation with known ε, the method needs testing in actual applications where ε is unknown. [Gap] Real-world performance with estimated hyperparameters needs evaluation. [Decision] To achieve real-world validation, chose to use BeiDou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) SPP to perform positioning on IGS station data, expecting to obtain positioning RMSE under different geometry and gross error conditions.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Positioning accuracy of different methods shows different trends as size and rate of gross errors change; DIA-MAP RMSE can always decline to lower bound as gross error enlarges; when ε=10^-1 and ∇_i=50σ_i, DIA-MAP leads to improvements of around 71%, 60%, and 23% over DIA-datasnooping, DIA-p value, and iterative DIA-datasnooping respectively

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Multiple DIA methods exist for outlier handling including DIA-datasnooping, iterative DIA-datasnooping, and DIA-p value. [Gap] Need to demonstrate superiority of proposed DIA-MAP over conventional methods. [Decision] To achieve comparative validation, chose to use comparative analysis to perform performance comparison on positioning RMSE and detection metrics, expecting to obtain quantitative improvement measures.

parameters.comparison_methods: LS, DIA-datasnooping, iterative DIA-datasnooping, DIA-p value, DIA-MAP

02_agent_trajectory.observation: DIA-MAP always shows higher resistance to effect of multiple outliers than conventional DIA methods, especially under large gross error rate; robustness of conventional methods severely degrades as rate and magnitude of gross errors increase; DIA-MAP maintains satisfactory accuracy and stability

references.citation: Teunissen 2018; Zaminpardaz and Teunissen 2019, 2020; Yang et al. 2021
Lehmann and Lösler 2016; Teunissen and Montenbruck 2017

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Monte Carlo Simulation and Real GNSS Data Testing

TPR_improvement.interpretation: DIA-MAP achieves highest correct detection rate for outliers regardless of rate and size of gross errors

TNR_performance.value: stable between DIA-datasnooping and iterative DIA-datasnooping
TNR_performance.interpretation: DIA-MAP maintains balanced acceptance ability for inliers

RMSE_improvement_at_high_error.interpretation: When ε=10^-1 and ∇_i=50σ_i, DIA-MAP leads to 71% improvement over DIA-datasnooping

RMSE_improvement_over_p_value.interpretation: When ε=10^-1 and ∇_i=50σ_i, DIA-MAP leads to 60% improvement over DIA-p value

RMSE_improvement_over_iterative.interpretation: When ε=10^-1 and ∇_i=50σ_i, DIA-MAP leads to 23% improvement over iterative DIA-datasnooping

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was successfully achieved. The DIA-MAP method demonstrates superior True Positive Rate (TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) compared to conventional methods (DIA-datasnooping, iterative DIA-datasnooping, and DIA-p value) when dealing with multiple outliers in GNSS positioning data. Experimental results verified that DIA-MAP provides a unified DIA process for both single and multiple outliers, with positioning errors that can always be controlled even in cases of gross errors with high magnitude and proportion.
